title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Business People (Published January 22, 2001) The Alaskan Chapter of the American Society for Industrial Security has announced its board of directors for 2001. Chairman is Joseph Young, business manager for the Alaska Peace Officers Association; vice chairperson, Madeline Schatz, cargo security specialist with the Federal Aviation Administration; secretary, Charlene Derry, manager of Office of International Aviation with FAA; Bill Rodasky, safety and security manager with the Anchorage Hilton Hotel. Debbie Hedlund of AAA Travel earned the professional designation of Certified Travel Association from the Institute of Certified Travel Agents. To earn the designation, she completed an educational program covering geography, sales and service, communications and technology, and travel in…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bering air costs crab their legs RESEARCH: Grad student finds wind chill often too cold. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published January 18, 2001) Imagine being dragged into such cold that your legs pop off. That's what happens to thousands or even millions of snow crab each winter during commercial fisheries in the Bering Sea. It's a big problem for crabbers and a puzzle for Jonathan Warrenchuk. The University of Alaska Fairbanks graduate student has completed some unusual lab experiments that could keep more crabs walking and cut down on crab waste during commercial harvests. This week more than 200 boats are expected to set sail on the frigid Bering Sea to catch 27 million pounds of snow crab, the state's most valuable crab fishery. In the process, lots of crabs are sure to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Fishery won't be limited COPPER RIVER: Dipnetters vow to keep fighting for more early Copper River salmon. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published January 16, 2001) The Alaska Board of Fisheries unanimously rejected a proposal Monday to limit the Copper River commercial salmon fishery to allow more fish to swim upriver for Interior dipnetters. The vote relieved commercial fishermen from Cordova, who said the proposal could have wrecked the value of their fishery and the local economy. Dipnet proponents vowed to keep fighting. "You can tell the people in Cordova not to go buy new boats yet," said Stan Bloom, vice president of the Fairbanks-based Chitina Dipnetters Association. "We're going to see what the courts have to say about not providing fish for subsistence." Marketers ha…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Halibut catch limits may climb Statewide restriction for commercial fishermen under review The Associated Press (Published December 19, 2000) Anchorage -- Commercial halibut fishermen who hold individual fishing quotas could see an increase in their catch limits. The International Pacific Halibut Commission is reviewing a staff recommendation that would increase the statewide limit to about 63.5 million pounds in the 2001 season, up about 10 million pounds from last year. Commission Director Bruce Leman expects commission members to approve the staff recommendation when it meets in January. Under a staff recommendation, the biggest increases for individual fishing quotas would be in areas 3A and 3B in the western and central areas of the Gulf of Alaska. Each of those areas would get …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | (Column) State considers delaying start of Copper River fishery By Laine Welch TY: Column (Published December 17, 2000) Kodiak -- The state Board of Fisheries will face a proposal to delay the start of the Copper River salmon fishery for subsistence reasons. Fairbanks-based dipnetters are requesting that the commercial fishery be delayed until early June to allow 100,000 more reds and kings to get upstream. According to state estimates, that means an additional 200,000 to 300,000 fish will likely be lost to the fishery. The Copper River's early runs make headlines around the world as Alaska's first salmon of the season; the anticipation is so great that buyers deploy helicopters so they can have the "first fish" for their customers. Not ones to take the estimated loss of $13.6 millio…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Researchers may hold key to Kodiak's fishing future By Laine Welch TY: Column (Published December 3, 2000) Kodiak -- They laughingly refer to themselves as pooper scoopers and fish squeezers, but their unglamorous work could hold the keys to Kodiak's fishing future. Nearly 95 percent of the Kodiak fleet's known pollock grounds are closed to fishing, as are most of the major cod grounds and all of the areas for shallow water flatfish. The fishing closures are designed to protect the habitat and dietary needs of dwindling numbers of Steller sea lions, but in fact no one really knows why the animals are disappearing. While federal fish managers fight out the details in court, University of Alaska marine mammal biologist Kate Wynn and fish scientist Bob Foy have been quietly pursuing wha…

title: Get your life back from work
summary: If there was one thing Bill couldn't stand, it was running behind schedule. Deadlines were the pegs on which he hung his life. A successful manager, he'd started with nothing and made it to senior management because of his drive and self-discipline. He ran his life by the book and worked late every night. He left the office only when his wife called and volunteered to send him a sleeping cot via UPS or the janitor's vacuuming ruined his concentration. And then, one day, he realized his employees and co-workers routinely left work at 5 p.m. and, unlike him, socialized after work, worked out regularly and managed to attend their children's basketball games. Bill thought back on his last several months and realized his work consumed so much time it left him little energy for living. In short,…

title: Alaska business optimistic about 2001 growth despite worries over oil
summary: As the new year opens, Alaska business people are looking to the future. Oil prices are up. Construction is booming. Opportunity seems to be waiting. To tap the feelings of the business community as the clock clicks forward, the Daily News sent surveys to a variety of Southcentral Alaska business leaders. For some, the price of oil is the barometer of Alaska's economy. For others, oil is old school. For everyone who answered our survey, managing growth is a common theme. Some may be grappling with expansion Outside. Others are struggling to keep qualified workers or to break into new markets. Others are putting the damper on rapid expansion. In general, they are as a group optimistic about 2001, with some concern about the state economy's dependence on one resource: oil. Mayfield Evans is …

title: Conflicting data add uncertainty
summary: Kenai -- Conflicting sets of data have muddied the Cook Inlet red salmon forecast for next year. Numbers released by the Alaska Department of Fish and Game contain a 1.5 million-fish "fudge factor" because two models were used for the estimate, according to Mark Willette, a department research project leader for upper Cook Inlet. In numbers released on Wednesday, Fish and Game officials in Soldotna forecast a total Kenai River sockeye run of 2.4 million. For all of upper Cook Inlet, the total run is predicted at 4.2 million sockeye, with an escapement goal of 1.5 million and a harvest estimate of 2.7 million. Paul Shadura, vice president of the Kenai Peninsula Fishermen's Association and a setnetter on Kalifornsky Beach, said the run looks positive. "That would be much better than last yea…

title: Texas firm gets BP project
summary: BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. announced this week that a Texas company will do the construction work on an experimental natural gas liquefication plant in Nikiski. Austin Industrial is a Houston-based construction firm with experience building refineries, said BP spokesman Ronnie Chappell. The construction will amount to about $12 million of the $86 million plant. Anchorage-based Udelhoven Oilfield Systems Service Inc. will do electrical and instrument work on the project. That portion of the contract is worth $8 million. The remainder of the money for the plant is going to purchase land in Nikiski, site preparation, engineering and supplies. BP is building the plant to test new technology developed with Kvaerner, an international engineering firm, that turns natural gas into a liquid form.…

title: Natural gas prices soar
summary: Natural gas prices soared to a record Monday as a snowstorm socked the Midwest and bitter cold was in the forecast for at least the next few days. The soaring prices could almost immediately show up as increases in consumer heating bills, analysts said. Prices were up as much as 13 percent, hitting a high of $9.65 per 1,000 cubic feet in regular trading on the New York Mercantile Exchange after surging as high as $9.86 in electronic trading overnight. Phil Flynn, vice president and senior energy analyst at Alaron Trading Corp. in Chicago, said a storm that was expected to dump up to a foot of snow on Chicago raised fears that utilities may not have enough natural gas stored up to get through a harsh winter. Cold weather in the Pacific Northwest has also contributed to the rise in prices, h…

title: Kenai Logger Abandons Kiln-dried Lumber Sales
summary: After a catastrophic fire destroyed his sawmill last May, Kenai Peninsula logger Kevin Gates is back in business. But the owner of Kasilof-based Gates Construction is no longer selling kiln-dried lumber to Anchorage hardware stores. Gates, 49, has turned his attention to marketing wood chips to a Japanese firm while continuing to sell pulp logs to a Canadian mill that churns them into newsprint bought by several Alaska newspapers, including the Daily News, the Peninsula Clarion, the Homer News, Juneau Empire and Ketchikan Daily News. Gates is proud that Kenai trees are being used by Alaska businesses. "It's a very good feeling because it's adding value to the local economy," he said. Gates' chipping operation started Oct. 1. His business plan calls for selling up to 200,000 tons of wood ch…

title: Mining group building bridges
summary: Spokane, Wash. -- An industry that digs holes in the ground must adapt to new sensibilities in a dot-com world, mining leaders said Tuesday. "The public's attitudes have changed, and our industry needs new approaches and new solutions if we are going to have a viable North American mining industry in the 21st century," said Laura Skaer, executive director of the Northwest Mining Association. The association's annual meeting runs through Friday. "We've got a lot of things right in the last decade, primarily on the economic side." said Joe Danni, an executive with Placer Dome America. "The question is, how do we get it right on the social side?" The extraction industry must find a way to integrate what it does with social and environmental expectations, said Danni, who chaired a session on s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Buyout may aid crabbers Stevens urges U.S. to purchase boats By Wesley Loy Daily News Reporter (Published December 19, 2000) Alaska crab fishermen are hoping a $100 million boat buyback program pushed through Congress last week by Sen. Ted Stevens might save them from financial ruin because of crashing crab stocks in the Bering Sea. The hope is that maybe a quarter of the fleet of about 260 boats will take the buyout, leaving more crab for the remaining vessels. Stevens, R-Alaska, chairman of the Senate Appropriations Committee, included the plan as part of a package of legislation he demanded as the lame-duck Congress wrapped up its final budgeting last week. Stevens at one point even threatened to shut down the government to get his priorities. Buying back boats has proved an imper…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Stevens, administration strike balance on sea lions By David Whitney Daily News Washington Bureau (Published December 16, 2000) Washington -- The last roadblock to a budget deal was broken Friday afternoon when Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens and the White House compromised on a rider to protect the endangered Steller sea lion while minimizing the impact on commercial fishermen off the Republican senator's state. The agreement, which in essence phases in any new restrictions, cleared the way to final enactment of a package of spending and tax measures Friday night and the final adjournment of the lame duck congressional session. Stevens, chairman of the powerful Senate Appropriations Committee, had threatened to keep Congress in session indefinitely, and maybe deny the Clinton administration…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Salmon, herring targeted Agency urges new fishing limit so sea lions have balanced diet By Wesley Loy Daily News Reporter (Published December 2, 2000) Alaska's commercial salmon and herring fisheries might need to be restricted along with the giant pollock fishery to stop the steep decline in endangered Steller sea lions, federal officials said Friday. Steller sea lion: More than Pollock. (Daily News file photo) The huge sea lions need a balanced diet and might not be getting it because of competition from fishing nets, hooks and traps, according to the government's "leading hypothesis" for the 80 percent slide in Stellers in Western Alaska. The prospect of expanding sea lion protections to salmon and herring harvests chills the fishing industry, which over the last two years has bee…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bush would benefit Alaska delegation By David Whitney Daily News Reporter (Published November 9, 2000) Washington -- If George W. Bush can hold on to his apparent victory in Florida, his election would create the most hopeful future Alaska has enjoyed in Washington, D.C., since statehood. It would open up opportunities for the state's all-Republican congressional delegation to work on a host of natural resources issues with a like-minded Republican in the White House. Complete election coverage But even if Bush prevails, Tuesday's election could prove bittersweet for the state because of Republican losses in Congress, particularly the Senate where the chamber may be evenly split between Republicans and Democrats. That 50-50 division will not cost Alaska's two senators their committee…

title: WILLIWAW: Nighttime storm pounds Portage; rainfall at glacier visitor center tops 5 inches.
summary: A tremendous storm walloped Portage and Turnagain Arm with driving rain and hurricane-force winds Thursday night, and in the process destroyed buildings, toppled trees and knocked out power. Friday's wind buffets Samantha Clymer, a Portage Glacier Lodge employee, as she picks insulation out of vegetation near the lodge. Roofing material and sheet metal were blown hundreds of yards. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) No one reported injuries, but inconvenience ruled as rain slicked and puddled icy city streets and the wind in some quarters raged. A weather station near Arctic Valley recorded peak winds of 129 mph, said Dave Goldstein of the National Weather Service. The wind peaked at 75 mph at Glen Alps and about 35 mph near Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport. A snowslide blocked …

title: Fermented beaver blamed as 13 fall ill
summary: Thirteen people from the village of Manokotak were hospitalized Thursday after eating fermented beaver tails and feet apparently contaminated with botulism. The patients were picked up by plane and admitted to Kanakanak Hospital in Dillingham between 10 a.m. and 1 p.m., said hospital spokesman Bill Burinda. From Dillingham, two patients were then transported to the Alaska Native Medical Center in Anchorage for more intensive care, he said, and a third patient was flown to Anchorage on Thursday night. The patients in Anchorage are Lillie Pauk, in her late 60s; her daughter, Nancy Sharp; and Carl Gamechuck, 43. All were in stable condition, a hospital spokeswoman said. The names of the patients in Dillingham were not available. A sample of the fermented beaver was sent to the U.S. Centers fo…

title: Rumsfeld backs missile defense
summary: Washington -- Donald Rumsfeld, President-elect George's Bush's nominee for defense secretary, declared his support for a national missile defense system Thursday but gave little hint whether the new administration would look beyond a limited Alaska-based system planned by the Clinton White House "There is no question but that I think we should deploy a missile defense system when it's technologically possible and effective," Rumsfeld said at his confirmation hearing before the Senate Armed Services Committee. But Rumsfeld, who served as defense secretary under President Ford, did not add to campaign statements by Bush that he might expand the missile shield to protect not just the nation's 50 states as currently envisioned, but also its European allies and U.S. troops deployed around the g…

title: By Elizabeth ManningAnchorage Daily News
summary: The frozen mud flats of upper Cook Inlet don't look like they would harbor anything living. A flock -- or smoke -- of Pribilof rock sandpipers rises in unison from Beluga Flats. They breed on the Pribilof Islands, St. Matthew Island and Hall Island, arriving in Cook Inlet in late October and staying through March or early April. (Photo by Bob Gill) In this surreal, desolate marine landscape, ice chunks as small as cardboard boxes or as large as minivans shift about in silty waters the color of chocolate milk. A white band of ice and snow obscures the line between land and sea. But look closer. From a Cessna 206 skimming a few hundred feet above the mud and ice, two federal biologists on Wednesday scanned the sky for white-gray clouds they call "smokes." The smokes are flocks of shorebirds,…

title: After a half-day of mourning, family gets 'dead' brother back
summary: The policeman who knocked on Lauretta MacBeth's door at 1 a.m. Wednesday to say her brother had been found dead in a downtown alley was "a real sweet guy" but that didn't make the news any easier to bear. Daisy Clark smiles at her boyfriend, Neil Beaton, who hadn't died. Police had told the family that his corpse had been identified. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) "I was shouting, 'I can't believe this. What do you mean you found him?' "You don't want to believe it," MacBeth said. "It's horrible." The identification was positive, the officer said. Her brother Neil Beaton Jr.'s wallet and ID were found on the body, the photos matched, and a police officer who had words with Beaton a week ago recognized him. MacBeth hugged the policeman when he left and began the awful chore of calling…

title: Girls' post-hockey game fight leads to 20 suspensions
summary: A fight between two Chugiak High School students after a school hockey game Saturday has led to suspensions of some 20 students, and the number could grow, Anchorage School District officials said Tuesday. Two girls had a run-in at the hockey game between Chugiak and East High and then fought at a student's residence in Eagle River, said Jim Taylor, acting executive director of high schools. The other students suspended were watching the fight, he said. Taylor didn't know what started the dispute, which began at an evening hockey game at the McDonald Center ice rink in Eagle River. "I don't know if it was a boyfriend-girlfriend thing or what," he said. The girls apparently went to a nearby park to fight but then moved to a student's residence. He didn't know if the girls suffered any injur…

title: 2 face charges in dancers' case
summary: A Chugiak man and a Russian are in jail today, charged with lying to get seven Russian dancers into Alaska for "cultural" performances that turned out to be stripping naked at a local nightclub. Immigration agents arrested Tony Kennard of Chugiak on Tuesday afternoon on a felony count of aiding and abetting preparation of false visa applications for the seven women, who range in age from 16 to 30. Kennard told the Immigration and Naturalization Service that he brought the women to Alaska to perform Russian folk dances at a series of events in Anchorage, Eagle River and Homer. The day before, agents arrested Viktor Virchenko at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport as he attempted to fly back to Russia from Anchorage with three of the women. The four others had been taken into custody…

title: Seward Peninsula farmers want U.S. aid as reindeer run away with caribou
summary: State officials are seeking federal disaster funds for more than a dozen reindeer farmers on the Seward Peninsula whose animals are running off with the local caribou. The Western Arctic herd has grown to immense size, more than 400,000 animals, and is spreading west along the Seward Peninsula, encroaching on the herders' grazing lands. Once the reindeer mix with their wild cousins, they won't come back. An estimated 12,000 reindeer, about half the locally managed livestock, have joined the caribou herd in the past decade. Another 3,000 may have run off this winter, said wildlife biologist Greg Finstad, who manages the reindeer research program at the University of Alaska in Fairbanks. If the latest numbers are right, there may be as few as 9,000 reindeer left, compared to nearly 25,000 a …

title: Railroad: Prevention is priority
summary: The events of the past year have driven home an important point at the Alaska Railroad. "No matter what you spend on safety, it's cheaper than $9.3 million." The speaker is Ernie Piper, whose job is making sure the state-owned railroad's trains operate safely. The $9.3 million is roughly what the railroad has spent so far in its effort to clean up jet fuel spilled in a 15-tanker derailment at Gold Creek 12 months ago. Less than 15 percent of the 120,000 gallons spilled has been recovered, and remediation is expected to continue for years. The Gold Creek spill was the second in a series of accidents, leaks and misfortunes that pushed the railroad into the headlines and under the microscope in the past 18 months. Partly at their own initiative and partly because of pressure from lawmakers, o…

title: Rare winter wildfire roars over frozen federal refuge
summary: Firefighters are monitoring a rare, 20,000-acre fire burning on frozen tundra about 20 miles east of Kotlik on the southern coast of Norton Sound. Alaska's wildfire season is usually long over by December. "I haven't heard of anything like it," said Andy Williams, spokesman for the Alaska Interagency Coordination Center. The fire in Southwest Alaska is within the Yukon Delta National Wildlife Refuge managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. Larry Vanderlinden, fire management coordinator for the agency, said fire crews will be brought in only if the fire threatens Kotlik residents or other people and structures. Vanderlinden said that's not likely. Both the Pastolik and the Pastoliak rivers, as well as smaller streams and lakes, stand between the village and the fire. Observers have s…

title: Doors open anew for cribbage clubs
summary: Deal those cards. Count those hands. Move those pegs. Cribbage is back. State gaming officials say cribbage clubs may resume their weekly card games at the American Legion as long as no prizes are paid out. "We're not focusing on what you might call harmless activities," said Dan Dickinson, director of the division that oversees gambling permits. "But the statutes say certain things that must be followed. . . . You can't allow prizes of any size." During an inspection tour of American Legion Post 1 last month, a state gaming inspector spotted stored decks of cards and cribbage boards and warned that it all added up to illegal gambling. Word quickly spread to Post 28. Both posts hosted weekly cribbage clubs. Both posts reluctantly asked the cribbage players to take their games elsewhere. Th…

title: Every face is a mystery
summary: Michael Palmer was riding a bicycle in a subdivision near Wasilla. Jonnie Renee White was sitting in a pickup on a Kodiak beach. Magdalena Perez was being dropped off at her Anchorage apartment. These are the final reports, the last known sightings of three Alaskans. The three are among several dozen missing Alaskans whose photos, descriptions and circumstances appear on an Alaska State Troopers Web page, Active Missing Persons Bulletins. Troopers have been publishing an online list of missing people, the Missing Persons Clearinghouse, for more than two years. The bulletins began appearing in August. They are being added at a rate of about nine a month. Posted on the site as of Monday were the photos of 46 men, women and children, ages 4 to 84, who have been missing in Alaska from as far b…

title: Foreign ad has salmon industry rolling with laughter, maybe in sales
summary: Imagine Jackie Chan vs. the grizzly bear, facing off on a gravel bar. They punch, they twirl and kick, they scream bloody murder, fighting over a fresh red salmon. The John West Red Salmon commercial is the most requested video clip on the adcritic.com Web site. Click here to view the video. (Note: The commerical is a 2MB file that requires a Quicktime player .) Although filmed in the Scottish highlands and aimed at a British audience, this canned-salmon commercial has sparked conversation and laughter across Alaska and the salmon industry. It's not being broadcast here, but that hardly matters, as fast as it is flying by e-mail. "I've gotten it probably from 15 or 20 sources," said Sue Aspelund, executive director of Cordova District Fishermen United. "People love it." The ad for John Wes…

title: 43 more students suspended
summary: Forty-three more students were suspended Wednesday from Service High School for their role in a fight -- caught on videotape last week -- that left a boy unconscious and bruised. That brings the suspensions to 75. More are likely today, school officials said. School and police officials say the spectator aspect of the Dec. 5 fight -- and others like it -- marks a new and disturbing trend. A crowd estimated at 150 yelled and cheered as two boys punched it out until the younger and smaller one was knocked out. The video, aired Tuesday on KTUU-TV, Channel 2, showed the boy lying still in the snow. No one made an effort to stop the fight; someone helped him when it was over. Meanwhile Wednesday, near Dimond High just before noon, a police officer found a crowd of 50 students watching a fight. …

title: Construction booms in Anchorage
summary: A feeling of optimism is in the air, and it can be heard from the sound of pounding hammers, seen in new buildings popping up around Anchorage and felt in the overtime budget of the city's building department. This has been one of the busiest years for construction in Anchorage since the oil-driven boom of the early 1980s. And there's no sign that it is slowing as high oil prices and the prospects for a North Slope natural gas pipeline fuel the confidence of developers. "You have to go back to the 1980s" to find that kind of optimism, said Dick Cattanach, executive director of Associated General Contractors of Alaska. Neal Fried, a state labor economist, is not sure whether talk of the gas pipeline has spurred construction but said "it is certainly on the radar screen" of developers. A num…

title: Knowles aide quells rumors of Cabinet spot
summary: Juneau -- They were fraternity brothers at Yale, talk occasionally on the telephone and usually hook up at governors conferences, but the relationship between George W. Bush and Gov. Tony Knowles ends there, a spokesman said Tuesday. Answering rumors that the Democratic governor was being considered for a Cabinet post in a new Republican Bush administration, deputy chief of staff David Ramseur said, "To the best of my knowledge, the governor was not offered nor has he pursued a job with Bush or Gore. "But he's flattered to have his name bandied about." Rumors of a Cabinet position for Knowles gathered speed last Friday when U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens told a reporter that the governor would be a wise pick should Bush win the presidency, said Connie Godwin, Stevens' press secretary. "One of the r…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Want a cabin this summer? Call now RESERVATIONS: A night in these rustic structures usually goes for $25 to $50 By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published January 14, 2001) In late December, while others scurried about taking care of last-minute holiday shopping, Mark and Leslie Kroloff watched the calendar for another reason. They knew they had to if they wanted to be on a Prince William Sound beach the last week of June. And on Dec. 21, exactly six months before their planned trip, one got on the phone while the other was on the computer to reserve a public-use cabin in the Sound. A day -- or perhaps just hours later -- and they might have missed out. Competition for many of the cabins is that stiff. If you're contemplating a leisurely weekend at a U.S. Forest Service, sta…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | State's long-term economic outlook not all that bright By David Reaume (Published January 14, 2001) I am frequently asked my opinion of the long-term outlook for Alaska's economy. Barring an in-depth study -- that someone other than me needs to pay for -- the best alternative is to look at the longer-term historical performance of states whose economies in the past resembled what Alaska's is likely to look like in the future. The following four states fit the bill: Montana, Wyoming, North Dakota and South Dakota. All are small, have hard winters and have no appreciable manufacturing base. In brief, they are northern, western and resource based. Clearly, none of these states has experienced the kind of monster stimulus that Prudhoe Bay brought to Alaska. But absent another find like t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Sitka logging planned Conservationists decry Native group's proposal The Associated Press (Published December 29, 2000) Sitka -- Sitka's urban Native corporation is considering a contract to log its corporate lands at Katlian Bay. There's about 30 million board feet of marketable timber there but only 15 million to 20 million board feet will be affected, Shee Atika President Bob Loiselle said. "Quite a bit will be helicopter logging or will only be partial cut," Loiselle told The Daily Sentinel in Sitka. The proposed timber sale is to Rayonier, a Washington company, Loiselle said. It would bring large-scale logging to within 20 miles of Sitka on Baranof Island for the first time in more than two decades. The Sitka Conservation Society, however, is expressing concern about the logging…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Trustees to sell fund plan Amendment aims to protect state's savings account By Wesley Loy Daily News Reporter (Published December 14, 2000) Alaska Permanent Fund Corp. trustees agreed Wednesday they need to quickly educate lawmakers and the public on a proposed constitutional amendment to better protect the state's $26.9 billion oil wealth saving account from inflation. Board member James Sampson, a former Fairbanks North Star Borough mayor and former state labor commissioner, said the proposed amendment is a "major, major public policy change" that could be derailed by detractors who might misunderstand it. "It doesn't take much," Sampson cautioned. The six-member board met by teleconference Wednesday to firm up language in the constitutional amendment, which they hope legislators …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Dividends hit banks, markets 'I feel like I'm dreaming,' CIRI shareholder says By Paula Dobbyn Daily News Reporter (Published December 29, 2000) It's a heady time to be a CIRI shareholder. Or a car salesman, banker, real estate agent or anyone else looking to move merchandise. "It was an answer to my prayers," says CIRI shareholder Allen Bismark-Nickanorka, who used part of his $50,000 dividend to buy a pickup. Bismark-Nickanorka, who wants to be a pastor, also plans to buy beds for two foster children and pay off car and credit loans. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Most of Cook Inlet Region Inc.'s 7,000 shareholders with direct deposit received $50,000 on Thursday. Others will start receiving the special dividend checks as early as today. "I feel like I'm dreaming," Victoria …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Business Meetings (Published November 20, 2000) MONDAY The Anchorage Chamber of Commerce and the Alaska Journal of Commerce are hosting the third annual "Top Forty Under 40" awards luncheon at noon at the Anchorage Hilton Hotel. The event recognizes 40 individuals under the age of 40 who share a commitment to business growth, professional excellence and personal integrity. This is a reservation-only event. Reservations were due Nov. 15. For more information, call 272-2401. TUESDAY The Greater Wasilla Chamber of Commerce will meet at noon in the Mat-Su Resort. Bill White, business editor for the Daily News, will discuss how businesses can get publicity. Call 1-907-376-1299. The Anchorage Bed and Breakfast Association will hold a general membership meeting and Thanksgiving potluck dinn…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Deal reached to limit ships' water pollution Murkowski needs aid from Stevens, Young By David Whitney Daily News Washington Bureau (Published October 26, 2000) Washington -- A deal has been reached to curb cruise ship dumping of sewage and wastewater in the scenic channels and bays of the Southeast Alaska panhandle. But for it to pass this year, Sen. Frank Murkowski, who brokered the deal, and state officials must persuade Sen. Ted Stevens, chairman of the Senate Appropriations Committee, to attach the provision to a must-pass spending bill. The move is necessary because House and Senate negotiators are stalled over a Coast Guard reauthorization bill to which the cruise ship provision would have been added. Whether an appropriations rider is possible may depend on Rep. Don Young, who…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Captitalize real estate holdings By Chris Stephens Staff (Published October 29, 2000) An important area of commercial real estate not often seen in Anchorage is the sale-lease-back of large corporations' real estate. The collective value of American corporate real estate totals $1.4 trillion and represents about 25 percent of total corporate assets. Many corporations are putting the value of their real estate to work in their business through sale-lease-backs. This information comes from various sources, including "Monetize Your Bricks and Mortar through a Sale-Leaseback" by Gordon J. Whiting, in the November-December 1999 issue of Mergers & Acquisitions magazine ( www.findarticles.com ). A sale-lease-back occurs when a company sells property and then leases back the facilities for a…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Cruise ships tank new tests Discharges exceed legal limits By Paula Dobbyn Daily News Reporter (Published October 24, 2000) A stew of heavy metals, fecal coliform and total suspended solids in excess of legal limits has turned up in the latest discharge samples taken from three cruise ships operating in Alaska waters, according to documents obtained Monday from the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation. The fecal coliform count in one sample exceeded 2.4 million colonies per 100 milliliter sample, according to the DEC. Federal law bars fecal coliform from being higher than 200 colonies in a sample that size, said Deena Henkins, DEC water quality chief. Fecal coliform is a bacteria found in human waste. In addition, di-n-butylphthalate, a chemical often used in plastics manu…

title: LACK OF CHUMS MAY FORCE MUSHERS TO KILL SLED DOGS
summary: Disastrous salmon runs on the Yukon River may spell death for hundreds of Alaska sled dogs this winter. Eagle resident Andy Bassich with a puppy, Colt. (Paula Dobbyn / Anchorage Daily News) Mushers in Eagle and other villages along the Yukon say the closure of the entire river for subsistence chum salmon fishing this year may force them to kill dogs rather than let them starve. Chums provide the basic diet for dog teams along the Yukon basin. "I know Dick Cook shot his dogs and there are a few other people in town that are talking about it now," said Eagle resident Andy Bassich, who has a kennel of 14 dogs he uses for hauling water, firewood and winter tourists. Bassich said he may get rid of his dogs after this winter because the stress of trying to keep them fed is getting to be too much…

title: STATE SUPREME COURT BACKS 2-BALLOT PRIMARY
summary: Two justices had to listen in by phone, but Michael O'Callaghan got to tell the full Alaska Supreme Court on Tuesday why he thinks the two-ballot primary scheduled by the state for Aug. 22 should be stopped. Michael O'Callahan argued unsuccessfully Tuesday before the Supreme Court that the two-ballot primary should be stopped. Forcing voters to choose between a Republican ballot and an all-others ballot denies them the right to vote for candidates regardless of party, infringes on the secrecy of the ballot and violates Alaska law, O'Callaghan said. His arguments failed to convince the justices. Six hours later the court issued a brief order allowing the two-ballot primary to go ahead. A full explanation of its reasons will be issued later, the order said. Although he is not a lawyer, O'Cal…

title: WUERCH DISCLOSES PLANS FOR CITY
summary: Mayor-elect George Wuerch on Tuesday unveiled a new city management plan that emphasizes team leadership and customer service. He said he hopes the new approach will discourage voters from adopting a tax cap initiative this fall. Wuerch also named 11 appointees, including a new fire chief, city attorney and budget director, as well as the director of a new office he said would make the city more user-friendly and accessible to new business. "I don't think I need to tell this group about the threat of a taxpayer revolt," he told Anchorage Assembly members in a Tuesday afternoon work session. "We need to develop trust." The announcements offer the first solid look at how Wuerch plans to run the city in the shadow of a 10-mill tax cap initiative that will appear on the November ballot. The me…

title: Nations ink polar bear pact
summary: After eight years of negotiations and cooperation among Native groups on two continents, the United States and Russia signed a treaty Monday that will govern the harvest of polar bears throughout the frozen ocean off Alaska's west and northwest coasts. Representatives from Alaska and Russian Far East Native hunting groups will help manage polar bears in the Bering and Chukchi seas. (Daily News file photo) The agreement would create an international commission to monitor and study the relentless marine predators in the Chukchi and Bering seas and then set a quota of how many bears could be safely hunted for subsistence by Natives in both countries. In what officials say makes the agreement unique, representatives from Native hunting groups in Alaska and the Chukotka region of the Russian Fa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Playing smart helps shopper get PlayStation2 By Kathleen Mccoy Daily News Reporter (Published October 27, 2000) Last week, Ace Ueeck, 25 and a cook at Red Robin in Anchorage, had two strategies for getting a Playstation2 when it was released at midnight Wednesday. He had a deposit down at Microplay, and he planned to camp outside a Wal-Mart, just as backup. Well, Ueeck got his Playstation2 but not the way he'd planned. He got off work at 9:30 Wednesday night and drove to the Wal-Mart on A Street. What he saw dismayed him. A line snaked from a bench outside the electronics department all the way into layaway. "There must have been 150 people," he said. "They only had 39 units, so I knew there was no way." He got back into his car and started driving around town. At Fred Meyer, he enco…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Tax cap opponents rebut cuts Group challenges specific proposals By Richard Mauer Daily News Reporter (Published October 27, 2000) Opponents of the statewide property tax initiative on Thursday ridiculed the claims that tens of millions of dollars of lost revenues from the tax cap could be painlessly recovered through efficiencies, privatization and a flat-rate income tax. Complete election coverage At a press conference in the Loussac Library called by Alaskans United Against the Cap, chairman Ernie Hall questioned how Tax Cap Yes came up with its lists of savings announced at a press conference earlier this week. He also challenged the tax cappers to explain how they came up with specific elements of the initiative, which voters will decide in November. For instance, the initiative…

title: Panels forecast crowded, riskier schools, with conditions worse if tax cap passed
summary: Classrooms will be more crowded and schools will be dirtier and less safe, with fewer teachers, counselors, security officers and custodians, if the recommendations of citizen committees for reducing the Anchorage School District budget are put into place. Complete election coverage "I'm pretty depressed," acting Superintendent Carol Comeau said after the committees presented reports Wednesday night. "We have a large, large task ahead of us." The district asked for recommendations on two levels of cuts: 10 percent just to deal with rising costs, and another 10 percent in case the tax cap initiative is passed. The actual cuts required would likely be lower, but these proposals would give the district choices, Comeau said. The rising costs are mostly from raises for teachers and other employ…

title: CHICAGO ANGLER HITS THE JACKPOT
summary: A child of Puerto Rican immigrants and a lifelong big-city resident, Chicago's David Chaparro never had many opportunities to go chasing after fish with rod and reel until a co-worker lured him north to Alaska on a vacation this summer. Hundreds of pounds of halibut and almost $32,000 later, he's hooked on fishing. "Fishing is going to be one of my hobbies," Chaparro said by telephone from the Windy City on Thursday. "I didn't have a hobby before." Though Chaparro has yet to collect his check from the Homer Jackpot Halibut Derby, he has been identified as the winner of the annual fishing competition. Derby officials said he will collect at least $31,802 -- the largest prize ever -- and it could go slightly higher. "We have two ticket vendors we can't get to yet," derby spokeswoman Linda Wi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 'CLUELESS' HUNTERS KILL MOOSE IN PARK BRAND-NEW RESIDENTS GET LOST; 'HE'S LEGAL ALL AROUND, EXCEPT FOR WHERE WE SHOT HIM AT (Published September 21, 2000) Under the supervision of Alaska Fish and Wildlife Protection trooper Lisa Alleva, two wayward moose hunters were cleaning up the scene of their crime Wednesday within sight of the Chugach State Park viewing platform at Glen Alps on the Anchorage Hillside. Wildlife trooper Lisa Alleva hauls out moose antlers and a leg from an illegal kill in the park. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) "We made a bad mistake," said Rock Reed as he knelt next to an illegally killed bull moose. "We had called the Game and Fish," he said. "They said the area we wanted to hunt was fine, but it just got all screwed up." A skinny man with graying hair a…

title: HISPANIC IMMIGRANTS TAKE TROUBLES TO RADIO JOURNALIST
summary: A hurricane wrecks Honduras. Seven thousand miles away in Alaska, Gil Sanchez's phone rings. A father goes into hiding after school officials ask for his children's immigration papers. Then he calls Gil Sanchez. Sanchez on his role of being an advocate for people: "Money and problems with the bureaucracy, language, cost, documents. I'd like to make it all better, but I cannot be a wizard for them and make it all go away. A baby dies in a traffic accident near Portage. The distraught mother wants her husband released from jail to attend the funeral. She calls Sanchez. Gilbert Sanchez is a news producer for public broadcasting, an award winning journalist and host of "Intercambios," a radio talk show in Spanish and English that airs Sunday nights on KSKA. Those are just his day jobs. By nigh…

title: FOR SOME, NOT APPLYING IS A MATTER OF PRINCIPLE
summary: For those few Alaskans opposed to the Permanent Fund dividend, Thursday may have felt a bit like being a nonbeliever on Christmas. This year's $1,964 check will be the biggest in history. But a lonely few do not accept checks. Ralph Kermit Winterowd 2nd objects to the dividend on principle. "I'd say it's dishonest," he said. His reasoning is that because he cannot enter his full middle name on the dividend application -- only the initial K -- his incomplete application could expose him to perjury charges. Winterowd also does not pay federal income taxes. He believes in a strict interpretation of the U.S. Constitution and that the government has been corrupted from original form into a giant corporation. With 585,000 Alaskans applying for the dividend, not everyone thinks like Winterowd. So…

title: YOU'RE IN THE MONEY: $1,963
summary: Juneau -- More than half a million Alaskans can pay for a snowmachine, a round of drinks in a really crowded bar or a few months' groceries with the largest dividend ever paid by the Alaska Permanent fund -- $1,963.86. Revenue Commissioner Wilson Condon announced the payout Thursday, giving the booming stock market of recent years credit for the growth of both the Permanent Fund and the payment the state will make to 585,800 eligible men, women and children next month. Direct deposits will be made Oct. 4, and checks will be mailed a day later. The fund grew from $14.9 billion in 1995 to nearly $28 billion this year, while the dividend doubled in the same period. Last year's dividend was $1,769.84 But Condon warned that the rapid growth may not continue. Because the payout is calculated usi…

title: 'CLUELESS' HUNTERS KILL MOOSE IN PARK
summary: Under the supervision of Alaska Fish and Wildlife Protection trooper Lisa Alleva, two wayward moose hunters were cleaning up the scene of their crime Wednesday within sight of the Chugach State Park viewing platform at Glen Alps on the Anchorage Hillside. Wildlife trooper Lisa Alleva hauls out moose antlers and a leg from an illegal kill in the park. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) "We made a bad mistake," said Rock Reed as he knelt next to an illegally killed bull moose. "We had called the Game and Fish," he said. "They said the area we wanted to hunt was fine, but it just got all screwed up." A skinny man with graying hair and black mustache, Reed trembled in his Walls Blizzard Pruf coveralls on a 50-degree fall day. He stopped often from the chore of butchering the moose to smoke c…

title: SLOPE PLANE CRASH KILLS 4
summary: Four people were killed and six injured Monday afternoon when a commuter airplane crashed at the North Slope village of Nuiqsut. Officials with the North Slope Borough Public Safety Department and the Federal Aviation Administration said the twin-engine Piper operated by Cape Smythe Air went down while trying to land at the village airstrip about 3 p.m. Nuiqsut, with about 500 residents, sits on the open Arctic tundra about 60 miles west of Prudhoe Bay and about 35 miles from the Beaufort Sea coast. The plane had stopped in Deadhorse and was to make a stop in Nuiqsut on its way to Barrow. Two who died, identified by authorities late Monday, were from Anchorage: Chris Gibson and Gerald Kost. The other two were pilot Leo "Jason" Ostendorf and Kenneth Leavitt, both of Barrow. While none of th…

title: AT&T PLANS WIRELESS NETWORK FOR ANCHORAGE
summary: Anchorage is one of the first places nationwide where AT&T Wireless is offering a new service that beams phone calls and high-speed Internet into homes, bypassing the wires strung throughout the city. AT&T calls it Digital Broadband and it could dramatically change the way telecom companies deliver communications, giving customers another choice on how they place calls and surf the Internet. Up to 20 towers citywide will relay calls and data signals between houses and AT&T's switching center. Phones and computers will still be wired to jacks inside the home. But outside, a small antenna, shaped like a pizza box, will connect devices to the wireless network, instead of routing calls through underground copper lines or television cable. The wireless network is under construction and AT&T pla…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | ALASKA LOSES INCOME LEAD THREE STATES CATCHING UP, CENSUS REPORT INDICATES By Paul Queary The Associated Press (Published September 27, 2000) Juneau -- Three other states appear to be catching up to Alaska in median household income, according to Census Bureau figures released Tuesday. As recently as three years ago, Alaska stood alone at the top. Alaska's median household income averaged $51,046 from 1997 to 1999, according to the report. That's the highest among states surveyed. But Maryland, New Jersey and Connecticut are so close behind that they fall within the survey's sampling error, making the contest a statistical dead heat. The figures use three-year averages because the national survey's sample is too small in any given state to produce reliable results for a single year. …

title: ALASKA LOSES INCOME LEAD
summary: Juneau -- Three other states appear to be catching up to Alaska in median household income, according to Census Bureau figures released Tuesday. As recently as three years ago, Alaska stood alone at the top. Alaska's median household income averaged $51,046 from 1997 to 1999, according to the report. That's the highest among states surveyed. But Maryland, New Jersey and Connecticut are so close behind that they fall within the survey's sampling error, making the contest a statistical dead heat. The figures use three-year averages because the national survey's sample is too small in any given state to produce reliable results for a single year. For the three-year period from 1996 through 1998, only New Jersey was on a par with Alaska's median income, which was $52,557 when adjusted for infl…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Inside Alaska Business (Published October 31, 2000) Radio tower complete An Anchorage broadcaster has completed construction of a 360-foot radio tower at Point MacKenzie, across Knik Arm from Anchorage. Anchorage Media Group should begin broadcasting from the new tower in three to four weeks, said Dennis Bookey, general manager. If plans go well, the company's existing tower at Abbot Road and Lake Otis Boulevard should be removed around the end of the year, Bookey said. That will clear the way for construction to begin on a proposed 170,000-square-foot Fred Meyer store at the site. Fred Meyer officials were unavailable for comment Monday. Construction of the store has been delayed since spring 1999 due to finding a suitable spot to relocate the broadcasting tower. Law firms altered W…

title: RVERS SAY FUEL COSTS CUT OTHER SPENDING
summary: Not everyone in Alaska is celebrating high oil prices. John Medoro, a retired engineer from Colorado Springs, Colo., can pay as much as $400 to fill the fuel tank of his mammoth RV. His converted motor coach needs to be refueled only every 1,000 miles or so: It stands 14 feet high, is 40 feet long and totes a 260-gallon diesel tank. But at four miles to the gallon, he'd rather not think about filling up - he just sends his wife in to pay. Gale Harroff writes in his diary while his wife, Barbara, pages through a visitors guide at Anchorage RV Park. Harroff said high gas prices haven't slowed his travel plans, which include spending a couple of weeks in town. "At first you grit your teeth," he said. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Soaring oil - and gasoline - prices are hitting tourists…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Ahmoagak takes helm of municipal gas line consortium (Published November 16, 2000) Fairbanks -- The mayor of the North Slope Borough has been named the new chairman of the municipal consortium that's pursuing a natural gas pipeline. Mayor George Ahmoagak succeeds former Fairbanks North Star Borough Mayor Hank Hove. Ahmaogak said he hopes to accelerate promotion of the Alaska Gasline Port Authority. "Alaskans have been waiting for a gas line project for years, and I think the day is near when this dream will become a reality," Ahmaogak told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. "When it does, it will be in the best interest of all Alaskans to have the port authority involved." Hove no longer is on the board. He moved to Nevada when his term as Fairbanks North Star Borough mayor ended in Oct…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | ANWR: RUMOR AND DESIRE By Ben Spiess And David Whitney (Published August 24, 2000) What two words send a chill through Alaska oil executives? Greenpeace? Sorry, that's one word. Try: National monument. Taking up former President Carter's call Wednesday to designate the coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge as national monument could put what may be the last great onshore oil bonanza in the United States out of reach. "To say the least, that would not be good for the Alaska oil industry," Kevin Meyers, president of Phillips Alaska Inc., said earlier this week. Rumors have flourished in the past three weeks that President Clinton will move to make ANWR a monument, although his administration denied the rumors. Monument status would be a big step toward protecting ANWR's …

title: Code clips business at Thrifty Barber Shop
summary: In the basement of his Fairview house, Don Goins will cut your hair for $13, and give you a "style cut" for $16. What he can't do is show off his red-, white- and blue-striped barber poles and awnings. The $5,000 awnings are now painted reddish brown and the poles are covered with gray garbage bags secured with duct tape. It's not his choice, Goins said, but the will of the "darned" city zoning enforcement officials who allow his home-based Thrifty Barber Shop to stay, but not the signs. "I'm losing trade like crazy," Goins said as he finished cutting what was left of a client's silver gray hair. "(Clients) call me all the time to see if I am still open after seeing the barber poles covered." Goins, who started cutting hair 45 years ago when a trim was $1.25, seemed resigned to the city's …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Seattle firm picks Soldotna for next hotel The Associated Press (Published October 6, 2000) Kenai -- A Seattle-based company plans to begin construction next summer on a 63-room hotel near the Kenai River in Soldotna. The Aspen Hotel, off the Sterling Highway, will open in spring 2002, said George Swift, president and chief executive of Western Steel. Western Steel has built more than 40 hotels in about 10 states, Swift said. It now owns and operates 12 hotels in Colorado, Montana, Washington and Alaska and employs about 150 people. It has done construction in Alaska since 1984. In June, Western Steel opened the 94-room Aspen Hotel in Juneau. It is building a 97-room Aspen Hotel in Fairbanks. Both cost about $5 million. Western Steel owns the Guesthouse Inn in Valdez. It built and so…

title: Rules aim to corral farmed salmon
summary: Victoria, British Columbia -- Fish farmers are about to face tighter regulations designed to prevent escapes of Atlantic salmon from fish farms in the province, a move they say they welcome. Besides the new aquaculture rules, provincial Fisheries Minister Corky Evans was set to announce several pilot projects to test more environmentally sensitive fish farming techniques. Ministry officials would not comment on the measures. "All equipment, materials and structures employed at a (fish farm) must be designed, constructed, installed, inspected and maintained so as to prevent escapes," the rules state without listing any penalties. The regulations, effective Oct. 31, require all British Columbia fish farmers to submit written escape and response plans that include measures for predator avoida…

title: TAKE NEW APPROACH WITH BOSS
summary: Question: I work for the original skinflint and I'm in charge of maintaining a computer system that badly needs regular infusions of money. I regularly battle with my boss over spending money on our computers and I'm frustrated to the point of quitting. I just put together the absolute minimum budget for keeping our system functioning for the next year. I need to ask her for $3,200. I know she'll tell me we need to put off spending that much money. My boss doesn't see that my negotiation skills and willingness to figure out a minimal fix allow us a solution for one-third the real cost. Unfortunately, when I try to show her this, all she sees are the dollar signs and she stops listening. She uses a 286 and has a secretary doing envelopes on a typewriter because our printer balks when we fee…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Outdoor Hotline (Published October 8, 2000) Gear up for snowshoeing World snowshoeing champ Tom Sobal will be at Recreational Equipment Inc. at 7 p.m. Tuesday to talk about equipment, clothing, route selection, technique and precautions. Sobal is technical director of snowshoeing for the Special Olympics. He has won more than 120 snowshoeing races at distances up to 100 miles. A young moose pauses while feeding. The calf was with its mother neat Ted Stevens International Airport last week. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Ski patrol needs help Skiers are needed for the Anchorage Nordic Ski Patrol. The patrol provides emergency services at local Nordic skiing events and promotes safety. Volunteers are trained in winter emergency care, survival, avalanche awareness, search and res…
